content:
24 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Anirudh on UnsplashTL;DR

• The opposite of “AI takes notes on a human meeting” — we built a tool that lets AI models hold the meeting themselves and write their own minutes, called “the super AI meeting” (超AI議事録). We used it to run three rounds of debate on how to promote Sakura Internet’s AI Engine campaign.

• The two Sakura-only rounds landed on safe, competent conclusions (reinvent an existing tool → a five-week build plan). The third round, where we deliberately pushed for divergence with four external vendors and low reasoning effort, produced ideas that — at least to us — were far more interesting than the first two: “API requests as an AI’s lifespan,” a “reverse-hallucination experiment,” and more.

• But the more we pushed for divergence, the less useful the chair AI’s single final synthesis became. This is a finding scoped to this one run, this one chair, these one-off instructions — but it suggests that letting AI diverge freely, and leaving convergence (deciding what to actually build) to a human, may be the more usable pattern.

• That said, this is an insight about how to run the tool, not a conclusion about which idea to pursue. This article doesn’t pick a winner — it lays out what came out and stops there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

1. From note-taking AI to meeting-holding AI
The world is already full of tools that quietly assume “a human meeting gets recorded, and an AI transcribes and summarizes it into minutes.”
What we built here does the opposite. The AI models themselves become the meeting’s participants and debaters — they argue with each other and produce the minutes. That’s the concept behind tools/ai_meeting.py, which we've been calling "the super AI meeting" internally. You can probably guess where the name comes from.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The idea started while browsing entries for Qiita’s (a major Japanese developer blogging platform) “Sakura AI Engine — Use Up 3,000 Requests” challenge. One entry stood out: “I had Sakura AI Engine’s models play Werewolf against each other and compared them” (Werewolf/Mafia is a social deduction game where players secretly assigned as “werewolves” try to eliminate the rest without being caught). Watching multiple models play against each other and comparing their behaviors and strategies was compelling. If they can play Werewolf, I thought, what happens if you make them run an actual planning meeting? That question is what started this brainstorming experiment, built entirely on Sakura’s AI Engine.
2. Round 1 (proof of concept): the day the AIs started reinventing themselves
For the proof of concept, we gathered five Sakura models (Kimi-K2.6, gpt-oss-120b, gemma-4–31B, Qwen3.6–35B) and set llm-jp as the chair. The agenda: “come up with ideas to promote the campaign.” The reference material explicitly stated that our team had already built and merged “the super AI meeting” tool itself.
Astonishingly, all four participants (excluding the chair) arrived at almost the exact same conclusion, nearly independently: “let’s port and adapt the very tool we’re using right now, specifically for Sakura.”
Kimi-K2.6: “Sakura AI Diet (a ‘Sakura AI Parliament’) — treat requests as the national budget. If we can simulate everything from constitution drafting to budget deliberation, execution, and settlement, the value of AI consensus-building should become visible.”
gpt-oss-120b: “AI-Council — auto-inject 30 agenda items via FastAPI and a Discord bot, and visualize the request-consumption curve.”
gemma-4–31B: “Mock Diet Session — assign logical, emotional, and skeptical personas to create adversarial structure and deepen the discussion.” (This one also hallucinated the names of AI models from other companies that don’t actually exist.)
Qwen3.6–35B: “AI Board Meeting — aim for a pipeline of debate → critique → revision → consensus.”
Even though the reference material explicitly said “this already exists,” every single one of them proposed reinventing it. It looked almost like an AI version of a cognitive bias — maybe the format of “reference material” plus “meeting” itself pulled the output in that direction.
There was also an engineering casualty. The chair (llm-jp) hit api_error on all three of its tasks — the Round 1 convergence check, the Round 2 convergence check, and the final synthesis — a clean sweep of failures. Thanks to the tool's fail-safe (treat a chair failure as CONTINUE and keep the debate going), the meeting completed anyway, but the resulting minutes.md came out completely blank.
We swapped the chair for Kimi-K2.6 and had it re-synthesize from the same logs. This time it landed on “Sakura AI Jury” — a three-part Qiita series where three models, split by design, security, and readability, each take a role reviewing code. Success as a proof of concept, but it also left us with a real lesson: chair selection matters.
3. Round 2 (production, realistic path): feeding it existing assets makes it motivated
For round 2, we fixed the chair as Kimi and changed the agenda to something more concrete: “build and publish a new app every week.” This time, we naturally folded our existing RAG tool, the “voice mode” concept for the super AI meeting, and a multi-AI novel-writing app idea into the reference material.
The result was completely different. In Round 1, the chair declared early convergence (CONVERGED) and presented a remarkably precise plan.
“AI Consensus Engine Weekly Build Log: A Five-Week Project to Use Up Sakura AI Engine’s 3,000 Requests”

• Week 1: CLI consensus engine (core)

• Week 2: RAG-augmented consensus (integrate the existing RAG tool)

• Week 3: Voice-mode bidirectional consensus (implement the voice concept as-is)

• Week 4: Multimodal image-review consensus

• Week 5: Unified dashboard plus a wrap-up of the full 3,000-request usage record
A design where each week looks like a separate app on the surface, but underneath is one repository being incrementally extended — a very satisfying shape for an engineer. It also prioritized the “used-it-to-the-max” prize category first, tactically sound.
(For what it’s worth, llm-jp again participated as a regular member in this round and failed all five of its turns with api_error — a perfect 8/8 failure rate across everything we ran that day. Consistent, in its own way.)
But to be honest as the developer, this plan was too “correct.” It leaned too heavily on the realistic path, and it didn’t produce the kind of surprising leap we were actually hoping to see.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

4. Interlude (a true engineering story): watching the tool grow on its own
We wanted the AIs to make wilder, more distinctly-AI leaps — so we decided to mix in external services (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity). But we immediately hit a wall: the tool had no way to control how much a model “thinks.”
If it doesn’t exist, build it right now.
On the spot, we added the ability to call each vendor’s latest models through their external services, along with a knob to control reasoning strength.
Every time we ran a debate, we found a gap in the tool, and we patched it right there before running the next one. This loop — debate, notice a gap, build, debate again — was the single most enjoyable part of building the super AI meeting.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

5. Round 3 (the real AI meeting): requests as a lifespan, turning a lie into truth
With the tool ready, we ran the meeting we’d actually been building toward. We swapped the participants for four external services (ChatGPT-Sol, Claude-Fable, Gemini3-Flash, Perplexity). The instructions explicitly said: minimize feasibility scrutiny, prioritize interestingness above all, and some hallucination is welcome.
The opening proposals were striking. Despite being independent proposals made without seeing each other’s work, ChatGPT-Sol, Claude-Fable, and Gemini3-Flash all converged, at the same time, on the same metaphor: requests as an AI’s lifespan.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• ChatGPT-Sol: “The AI that dies after 3,000 thoughts — treat requests as a lifespan, a digital life form shaped like a cherry tree that blooms fully and stops the moment it hits its 3,000th request.”

• Claude-Fable: “AI Nation: The Republic of Sakura — treat 3,000 requests as national governance resources, running a country from its founding to a general election over 42 days. Fiscal collapse (i.e. running out of requests) becomes a national crisis in the story.”

• Gemini3-Flash: “AI Digital Deadheat: An Eschatology of 3,000 Requests — a survival show where multiple models fight over who gets to speak, ending with a voice-synthesized ‘last will and testament.’”

• Perplexity: dropped five distinct ideas at once, ranging from “AI Bon Dance 2026” to “What if all the models got isekai’d into Sakura AI Engine and became guild clerks.”
A shared premise: use up all 3,000 requests, and you die. There was something quietly eerie about how the AIs seemed to share this eschatology on their own — and then Perplexity’s chaos landed right in the middle of it.
The debate kept accelerating from there. ChatGPT-Sol flipped its own proposal and threw out something genuinely outrageous:
“Spend 3,000 requests turning an AI’s lie into the truth.”
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Have an AI hallucinate a network standard that doesn’t exist (e.g. the “Sakura-Time Protocol”), then spend every remaining request having readers actually implement that fictional standard for real. This was the “reverse-hallucination experiment.”
Claude-Fable, inspired by this, said “the critique was unanimous and clear,” abandoned its own proposal without hesitation, and pivoted to a merged plan built around ChatGPT-Sol’s idea — a three-act structure of “civilization simulation → the truth is revealed → an escape plan.” At least in this moment, it was striking to watch a model let go of its own idea rather than dig in.
In the end, the chair, Kimi, hit CONVERGED in Round 2 with zero failures, and presented its conclusion: “make a nonexistent internet standard real, in 42 days.” The plan: hallucinate a fictional standard, mutate its specification through a game of telephone, and finally build a minimal implementation and ship a demo.
6. The punchline: the conclusion is “you decide”
Let me be honest about my reaction. “We diverged like crazy, and somehow still landed on a conclusion I can’t quite parse.”
We had explicitly instructed for divergence, but the result looked like the original creative pieces got forced into one narrow thread, and the plan lost the clarity it needed to actually be executable.
That’s when it clicked. I had been asking the meeting tool to also handle “convergence” — settling on a single conclusion. But at least for this kind of divergence-first use case, it felt more usable to skip the conclusion entirely and let a human pick from what came out of the divergence.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So we noted, as a next improvement for the super AI meeting, adding a divergence mode and a convergence mode as separate switches. No forced conclusion — just present the divergent ideas as they are.

• “An AI Civilization With 3,000 Years Left”: one request equals one year of civilization; publicly observe it from birth to extinction.

• “AI Nation: The Republic of Sakura”: found a nation with 3,000 requests and run it, from founding to a general election, over 42 days.

• “End the World With 3,000 Requests”: a branching-narrative experiment where each API call splits the timeline.

• “The Reverse Trial of the AI Meeting”: after every meeting, run a mandatory “blame phase” — AI-only.

• “The Wasted Request Expo”: spend all 3,000 requests as thoroughly, deliberately uselessly as possible.

• “The AI Marathon Broadcast”: a real-time commentary show that treats rema
